ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for crispy deep-fried Hong Kong egg noodles; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for crispy Hong Kong noodles; A0FJP (noodle-based dish) used as the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of crispy Hong Kong noodles is approximately 350 g, comprising a bed of deep-fried egg noodles topped with stir-fried seafood, pork, and vegetables in soya gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Crispy Hong Kong Noodles — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Crispy Hong Kong Noodles (cr_id F05060038). Iron value is reported as -1 (not available) in the source; all other macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Egg Noodle-based Dishes — analogous estimate based on egg noodles (GI ~58, medium) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crispy Hong Kong noodles in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous food: egg noodles (boiled), GI ~58, as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Deep-frying the noodles creates a gelatinised-then-dried starch structure that may slightly lower the effective GI relative to boiled noodles; however, the boiled egg noodle GI of 58 is retained as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 41.5) / 100 ≈ 24.1, based on 350 g serving providing approximately 41.5 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 262 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 917 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce during preparation, and avoid adding extra condiments to keep cumulative sodium within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (11.86 g/100 g) with medium GI (58) and low dietary fibre (1.08 g/100 g); a 350 g serving provides approximately 41.5 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~24.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit serving size to 200–250 g, request additional non-starchy vegetables such as chye sim to increase fibre and slow glucose absorption, and avoid sweet sauce variants that significantly elevate sugar content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat content (5.5 g/100 g) and generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs); high sodium (262 mg/100 g) also poses cardiovascular risk at typical serving sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions (≤200 g), preferring preparations with reduced oil and sauce, and pairing with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the atherogenic impact of the fried preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote systemic inflammation and worsen joint symptoms in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the deep-fried preparation; request steamed or boiled noodle alternatives to eliminate the AGE burden while retaining the seafood and vegetable components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Hong Kong Noodles is a popular Singaporean Chinese dish consisting of thin egg noodles that are deep-fried until golden and crispy, then served dry as a base topped with a stir-fried mixture of prawns, squid, crabsticks, sliced pork, and chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage) in a savoury soya and oyster sauce gravy. The dish offers a textural contrast between the crunchy noodle base and the moist, flavourful seafood-and-pork topping. Per 100 g, it provides 118 kcal, 5.19 g protein, 5.5 g fat, 11.86 g carbohydrate, 0.33 g sugar, 1.08 g dietary fibre, 262 mg sodium, and 23 mg calcium. Iron data is not available in the HPB source. The medium glycemic index (~58) and moderate carbohydrate content make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium level of 262 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management, and the deep-fried preparation generates AGEs that are contraindicated for rheumatoid arthritis. This dish is a staple at Singaporean Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char stalls, commonly ordered as a shared main course at family meals or working lunches. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean, mollusc, gluten, egg, or soy allergies.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060038), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The exact dish name matches the HPB entry. Iron is reported as not available (-1) in the source; all other macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using egg noodles as the closest defensible proxy, which reduces confidence for the GI component only; overall record confidence remains high due to exact HPB match for all other nutrients.
notes: iron_mg is set to -1: the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Crispy Hong Kong Noodles (F05060038) does not report an iron value for this dish. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this exact Singaporean preparation as of 2026. Calcium (23 mg/100 g) is preserved from the HPB source. The input record incorrectly classified this dish under primary_cuisine 'western' and dietary_tags 'plant_based'; these have been corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese' and the dish is classified as non-vegetarian containing seafood, pork, and egg noodles. The glycemic load per serving of 24.1 is calculated as (58 × 41.51 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains pork; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. The input GI source 'Embedded GI Database' is not an approved source; GI has been re-anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database using egg noodles (GI ~58) as the analogous estimate basis.
